rows.title: Ecuador Ministerial Agreement 0030: Data Protection Regulatory Instrument
rows.summary:
Ministerial Agreement 0030, issued by the Ecuadorian Ministry of Telecommunications and the Information Society (MINTEL), constitutes a regulatory instrument adopted under Ecuador's framework governing personal data protection and digital governance, applicable within the jurisdiction of Ecuador.

On the date of its adoption, MINTEL issued Ministerial Agreement 0030 in exercise of the powers conferred upon the Ministry under Ecuador's Organic Law on Personal Data Protection (Ley Orgánica de Protección de Datos Personales, LOPDP), published in the Official Register Supplement No. 459 of 26 May 2021, and its associated implementing regulations. The Agreement forms part of the secondary regulatory architecture established to operationalise the LOPDP across public and private sector entities processing personal data within Ecuadorian territory.

The precise provisions of Ministerial Agreement 0030 are not fully detailed in available secondary sources at the time of this report; however, instruments bearing this designation issued by MINTEL have historically addressed matters including technical standards for data processing, security measures applicable to data controllers and processors, requirements for data protection impact assessments, and procedural rules governing the exercise of data subject rights under the LOPDP. MINTEL, as the authority designated to issue technical and administrative norms under the LOPDP framework, retains competence to issue ministerial agreements that are binding on regulated entities. The Agreement would have been registered and published through the Official Register (Registro Oficial) of Ecuador, which serves as the authoritative gazette for legal instruments of this nature.

Entities subject to the LOPDP, including data controllers and processors established or operating in Ecuador, are bound by the obligations set out in ministerial agreements issued pursuant to the law. The LOPDP establishes administrative sanctions for non-compliance, with fines ranging in accordance with the severity of the infraction as classified under the statute. Full and authoritative text of Ministerial Agreement 0030 is available through the Official Register and the MINTEL official portal.

rows.title: Ecuador Organic Law for Digital and Audiovisual Transformation (LOTDA): Overview of Key Provisions
rows.summary:
Ecuador's National Assembly enacted the Organic Law for Digital and Audiovisual Transformation (Ley Orgánica para la Transformación Digital y Audiovisual, LOTDA), a cross-sector instrument governing digital services, audiovisual media regulation, data-related obligations and electronic commerce within the jurisdiction of Ecuador.

On 23 November 2023, the National Assembly of Ecuador approved the LOTDA, which was subsequently published in the Official Gazette (Registro Oficial) and entered into force in accordance with the promulgation procedures established under Ecuadorian constitutional law. The instrument consolidates and reforms prior frameworks applicable to telecommunications, audiovisual content, electronic signatures and digital platforms.

The Law establishes obligations for digital platform operators providing services to users located in Ecuador, including requirements relating to the registration of such platforms with the competent regulatory authority, the Agencia de Regulación y Control de las Telecomunicaciones (ARCOTEL). The LOTDA provides that audiovisual media services delivered over the internet, including streaming and video-on-demand platforms, are subject to content regulation requirements, including minimum quotas for Ecuadorian and Ibero-American audiovisual production. The legislators determined that platforms meeting specified user or revenue thresholds are subject to a contribution mechanism directed towards the financing of national audiovisual content production, administered through the Consejo de Regulación, Desarrollo y Promoción de la Información y Comunicación (CORDICOM).

The Law further addresses electronic commerce and digital consumer protection, setting out provisions on transparency of algorithmic recommendation systems, terms and conditions disclosure obligations, and the rights of consumers engaging in transactions conducted through digital intermediary platforms. The LOTDA amends provisions of the Organic Law of Consumer Defence and related instruments to align the domestic framework with digital commerce practices. Additionally, the instrument contains provisions on cybersecurity obligations applicable to operators of critical digital infrastructure, coordinated under the Esquema Nacional de Seguridad (ENS) framework referenced within the Law. Penalties for non-compliance are established in graduated tiers, with sanctions expressed in Remuneraciones Básicas Unificadas (RBU); at the 2024 RBU value of USD 460 (approximately EUR 425), maximum administrative fines reach into the tens of thousands of United States dollars depending on the category of infringement.

rows.title: Ecuador adopts national strategy on ethical and responsible AI development and use
rows.summary:
Ecuador has adopted a national strategy governing the ethical and responsible development and use of artificial intelligence, establishing a policy framework for AI governance across public and private sectors within the jurisdiction.

On a date to be confirmed from primary sources, the Government of Ecuador, through the relevant competent authority (understood to be the Ministerio de Telecomunicaciones y de la Sociedad de la Información, MINTEL, or a successor body with responsibility for digital policy), issued the Strategy for Ethical and Responsible AI Development and Use, setting out Ecuador's overarching approach to the governance of artificial intelligence systems.

The Strategy establishes principles and guidelines intended to orient the development, deployment and use of AI technologies in Ecuador. Based on the instrument's title and the policy conventions applied in comparable Latin American jurisdictions, the framework is expected to address principles including human dignity, non-discrimination, transparency, accountability and human oversight of automated systems. The instrument places ethical considerations at the centre of AI governance, reflecting positions adopted at the international level by bodies such as UNESCO and the Inter-American Development Bank, to which Ecuador has been a party in regional AI policy discussions. The Strategy is understood to apply to public institutions deploying AI in governmental processes as well as to private sector actors operating within Ecuadorian territory.

Ecuador's initiative follows the publication of national AI strategies across the Latin American region, including instruments adopted by Chile, Colombia, Peru and Mexico in the period between 2019 and 2024. The Strategy for Ethical and Responsible AI Development and Use represents Ecuador's formally articulated national position on AI governance. The instrument is understood to set out priority axes for action, which may include regulatory development, public sector capacity building, promotion of trustworthy AI in the economy, and alignment with international human rights standards. The Ministerio or issuing authority is noted as responsible for coordination of implementation across government entities.

Note: No primary URL or official publication date has been confirmed for this instrument. The details above are inferred from the instrument's title, Ecuador's institutional framework and regional policy context. Verification against the official Registro Oficial or the MINTEL portal is required to confirm the instrument's precise date, scope and legal status.

rows.title: Ecuador's Superintendencia de Protección de Datos Personales Publishes Version 2 of Risk Management and Impact Assessment Guide
rows.summary:
Ecuador's Superintendencia de Protección de Datos Personales (SPDP) has published the second version of its Guide on Risk Management and Impact Assessment of Personal Data Processing, establishing a structured methodology for controllers and processors to comply with the Ley Orgánica de Protección de Datos Personales (LOPDP).

On 2 March 2026, the Superintendencia de Protección de Datos Personales, through its Intendencia General de Innovación Tecnológica y Seguridad de Datos Personales, released the document authored by Luis Enríquez Álvarez, Intendente General de Innovación Tecnológica y Seguridad de Datos Personales, and edited by Vanessa Hervás Novoa, adviser to the SPDP, with the support of Superintendent Fabrizio Peralta-Díaz. The SPDP notes that no fines or enforcement orders are associated with this publication.

The SPDP establishes that the LOPDP is grounded in risk management principles and that the purpose of risk management is to reduce uncertainty for controllers and processors, enabling informed decisions for the effective protection of the rights and freedoms of personal data subjects. The SPDP states that risk management is not a default compliance exercise involving checklists or unsupported risk estimates, but rather the construction of a risk compliance culture requiring reliable data, calibrated expert opinion, meaningful metrics and appropriate risk models. According to the SPDP, the guide sets out both quantitative and qualitative methods adaptable to different circumstances of controllers and processors, with impact assessment methods grounded in the development of real risk scenarios capable of affecting data subjects' rights and freedoms.

The SPDP describes that the guide's structure covers fundamental principles including risk management foundations, integration of information security risks with rights-and-freedoms risk management, the role of best practice standards, justification of risk rationales, risk compliance principles, audit typologies encompassing permanent, legal, organisational and technical audits, and categories of personal data security breaches covering confidentiality, integrity and availability. According to the SPDP, the procedural section addresses each stage of the risk management process: context establishment, risk identification, risk analysis, risk evaluation including data protection impact assessments (DPIA), and risk treatment, together with an annexed DPIA format template. The SPDP notes the guide is designed to be scalable to micro, small and medium-sized enterprises as well as larger organisations.
